here is an URL with an  example on how enable tox with Gitlab CI

https://gitlab.inria.fr/msimonin/python-grid5000/-/blob/master/.gitlab-ci.yml

pre-commit profile and some other are missing.
I think it is more indeicated to rely on tox.ini envlist and just run "tox" (or even better "tox -p auto" to have // jobs if possible)

this is no more the only command use.

I think we should stick with tox.
I've reworked using printf

pre commit was doing:
* whitespace --> done in a specific shell script
* tabs --> done in a specific shell script
* gitlint --> done in a dedicated job

so I don't see the interest of using it.

Do I miss something?

I don't see the point of using tox to launch a few shell lines

I've created a script that does the same
no, it's just we don't need a "real" one ;)
sh -c is useless here out of tox.ini
fair enough

yes this is also intended to be installed in git hooks locally to perform modifications locall y and avoid loading the gate with back and returns.
cf tox profiles pre-commit-install and pre-commit-uninstall
With scripts the behavior is not guaranteed to be the same locally

+ Cédric

Outsourcing these commands from tox.ini to a dedicated script makes sense because of tox quoting hell issues and also to avoid direct calls to "sh" inside tox, what is a good thing from a virtualenv isolation perspective.

Though, IMO linters calls, even shell scripts, should better be centralized to a tox profile and this script should be called from tox.
This is highly wishable to reproduce CI behavior locally (moreover with python executables) but also for maintainability as already explained in https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/ci-management/+/124939 and https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/ci-management/+/124940.

One consequence of spreading linters configurations among several places is to forget repercuting some changes to a place as already observed in https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/ci-management/+/125018
From a sysadmin perspective, this also means installing new docker images for trivial scripts that should be runnable everywhere, what burdens the infra.
what about OOM-2643 instead ?

may I suggest we use another change to outsource these commands from tox.ini to a shell script declared in whitelist externals to improve the virtualenv current isolation ?

here are 2 examples
https://git.opendaylight.org/gerrit/c/transportpce/+/97384
https://git.opendaylight.org/gerrit/c/transportpce/+/96565/20/tox.ini#69

to be honest, I'm pretty sure that 99% of OOM contributors are not using them.

the one using them can still use tox + precommit hook to install them but we don't need to use them in the gate

Well I'm not in favour of that to be honest.

I understand this is your point of view but I do prefer the simplicity of using direct scripts instead of running them via tox. I don't see the value add.

checkbashims needs to be installed, even if we launch it via tox, the other scripts only needs git, curl, jq, find, gnu grep, sed and cut.

None of them are python based stuff so I think tox is not needed.

if you mean use this script in tox.ini, yes with pleasure.
if it's not what you mean, then I need a rephrase because I haven't understood ^_^
I'm not a sh specialist so I'm not sure what you want me to do but would it with pleasure

Everyone shoud use them before uploading code in my opinion.
These checks are just here to ensure it has been correctly done.

As it is written here, those scripts are not git hooks so the CI behavior are not ensured to be reproduced correctly on contributors system. More details in the next comment.

It may look simpler but I am pretty sure it is not at the end.

This is not only a matter of portability and maintenability.
The solution you propose here is adherent to gitlab-ci and is not ensured to work the same way locally on contributors OS, what makes any troubleshooting in the CI much more complex and longer since you need to run it on the same target OS than the CI, with docker for example.
Tox virtualenv is a much more lightweight solution.
Also as a result, OOM contributors are urged to upload their code to the repo to evaluate the results even for basic linting concerns, what leads to avoidable extra-loads on the CI infra.

With tox or pre-commit, it can work locally in any system and also in any CI (jenkins, gitlab-ci, github, drone-CI)  with a minimal configuration. You can even force concrete dependencies to ensure the same python packages are used without affecting the contributor operating system.

This is not the case with such scripts. Concrete dependencies are hidden and modifications made by the contributors to support them might affect their whole systems if they don't pass by a chroot/container environment. Moreover with GNU grep, sed etc, you can have issues with others UN*X variants. These issues can be much more complex than dealing with python hard and soft dependencies.

Of course, this explanation can be discussed for checkbashisms since it is not python based.
But checkbashisms use here is indeed a last resort workaround to have a linter that works with helm-altered scripts. I would have used the much more elaborate bashate python tool if I add more knowledge on helm to propose this other solution.
But please note this checkbahisms profile is the only exception in the current tox.ini configuration. All the other profiles in tox.ini are python-based tools (pre-commit*, gitlint, autopep8, docs, docs-linkcheck, doc8, pylint)

This solution is not adherent to gitlab ci:

* you want to check trailing whitespaces ? either install tox + precommit + whatever and run it OR just launch the shell script which use binaries present in 90% of developpers laptop
* you want to check tabs ? either install tox + precommit + whatever and run it OR just launch the shell script which use binaries present in 90% of developpers laptop
* you want to check gitlint ? either install tox + precommit + whatever and run it OR pip install gitlint and launch it (this will produce the exact same output)
* for documentation, we'll use the **exact** same process as tox BUT not in tox as we are in a docker

So again I don't see your point. If you want to use tox, fine, everything is present. But the CI won't.

yes this is what I mean.

note  #!/bin/sh whould be better than env sh, it is a UN*X convention

simply replace it by
! find . -not -path '*/\.*' -name '*.sh' -exec grep 'local .*=' \{\} + || exit 2

remove quotes and sh -c, they were a workaround for tox quoting hell issues
I've done both (and shebang stuff on all scripts)
Done

OK thx.
I will outsource this file in  another change and rebase this one on top of it so that we have the correct diff view to evaluate the gap.
ok !

Done

As stated I fixed a few other issues here and in the associated tox.ini.
I've also separated the Move to gitlab-ci from the migration to shell scripts in a third change.
I invite to rebase on top of https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/126205

For backwards compatibility, wouldn't it make more sense to keep a script in kubernetes/contrib/tools/check-for-staging-images.sh that still detects the GERRIT_BRANCH and calls .ci/check-for-staging-images.sh ?

As a matter of example, the -r grep option is not supported in some UN*X and BSD grep variants
for example OpenBSD one https://man.openbsd.org/grep
Their extended regular (-E) expression might also give different results than the GNU implementation implicitly used here
same GNUism present here

Also for a maintanibility stand point, I am not convinced it is wishable to have 2 different code at different places to perform the same actions.

If you want to evaluate the differences with the so-called equivalent script proposed here, I think it would be fair you try to use them at least a little.
LFN docs is not very verbose about that, so I wrote this little guide.
https://docs.opendaylight.org/projects/transportpce/en/latest/tox-guide.html
I invite you to read it.

I am quite surprised and sad to read that the OOM community is unhappy with the portable solutions I proposed to the point that they prefer rewriting them with non-portable scripts.

Sorry to insist but I don't see your point either.
I can't understand by which fancy, a configuration file named .gitlab-ci.yml could not be adherent to Gitlab-CI. Moreover with such shell commands destinated to be run on specific docker images maintained by Gitlab.
I must have missed something. And I can agree it is very likely the case since I am not a Gitlab specialist.
Please can you explain how you came to the conclusion this is not adherent to gitlab-ci?

IMO using python and tox virtualenv is first a matter of portability both for CI and OS.
This goal is very difficult to achieve with advanced shell scripts.
You can find more details in Rich's trick abstract at https://www.etalabs.net/sh_tricks.html

If portability is not your concern, I would be a little disappointed because I think it is a pity for OOM contributors. But we could agree to disagree on that point.
However, for that happens, this choice has to be clearly stated and assumed.

IMO  such apt-get commands can be very verbose and their output will have an added-value close to zero in such a context. I propose to use them with -qq and a few other tricks.
cf https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/126205

Also with the --yes option, installed packages might depend on a newer version of python3 that would be automatically installed.
I understand the image python:3.7 here is maintained by Gitlab but I don't know the details how.
So I cannot completely evacuate the risk that a python version different than 3.7 will be finally used at the end in this section.
Is this so problematic? Nothing is less sure 😊
Whether or  not, maybe a different image would be more indicated here.
I observed these commands lead to the installation of a lot of packages purposely removed by the Gitlab maintainers. And this takes a lot of time, about as long as the linters execution time itself...

I think only helm:fast is really required here.
The other linters are more about code style.
And they can fail on code that perfectly pass the other tests.
Maybe it is done on purpose but I am not sure of that.
Can you confirm it is really relevant to keep such dependencies here?

3.43 is actually a quite low pylint score.
Shouldn't we clean a little more the python file before activating this profile ?
I don't think pylint has been enforced yet
"use" rather ?

Such relative path script calls triggers an error.
They can only work with scripts at the root of the tox working directory.
Otherwise, {toxinidir}/ must be prepended to their paths.
I have fixed it in https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/126199.
Please rebase on top of its relation chain.

staging branch is not used for at least 5 releases

so it's more a cleanup that should have been done earlier

this will be used in a controlled environnment (the CI) where we're sure we have the right grep.

for other use, precommit is there for that.

this will be used in a controlled environnment (the CI) where we're sure we have the right grep.

for other use, precommit is there for that.
see my answer in main comment

well, of course .gitlab-ci.yml is adherent to gitlab ci but that's the way you configure it.

The docker images are not maintained by gitlab but are "any" images. I've decided to choose images that are either:
* "official" images of docker
* well maintained by a community
* done by us if not found

I understand your concern on the output and I'll add -qq
libenchant and curl doesn't have dependencies on python so no worries here.

I've thought about creating our own image for that but I've decided to stay this way and see if we have side effects.

nope, I don't want to waste "gating time" if bashisms / trailing whitespaces / tabs are present.

it'll need another change in the code and then instead of launching 2 gates, let's have a fast fail on this

set -eu can be removed
it is not portable and useless here because of all "|| exit" at the end of commands
Fair enough to do it here though I usually prefer this is done separately in a dedicated change

I would rather prefer you simply remove the precommit configuration.
This would avoid double maintenance and contributors might not be confused when they look at the place where this CI code is.
Especially in the case where precommit and this script behaviors diverge for some unclear reason.

In such a case and if I want to be 100% sure my change pass the CI, I need to use the docker image file specified in .gitlab-ci.yml.
Problem: docker is not supported by BSD systems... So I need first to run a VM with a GNU system to run docker or podman...

yes for curl and wget here but sorry, I forgot to mention this comment also (and mostly) applied to plantuml and graphviz at lines 233 and 234.

"-qq" is good but usually not enough. apt calls dpkg whose very verbose output can not be removed by -qq.
we shoud keep only stderr IMO

cf 16205.

Agreed there is no urge to create such an image in a first.
Though, please note python3.7 is already a bit old in debian-based systems and not only...

OK, if so, what about making helm:fast directly depend on these first group rather?

Note maybe helm:fast at the end or at the beginning of this list would make more obvious the clear separation of these 2 groups and the underlying will.

I prpose to stick with the original command with '{} +' found in in tox.ini
It was more portable and avoided different behavior depending on the OS system.
cf https://www.etalabs.net/sh_tricks.html

The code is currently rated 5.35 indeed.

I propose to outsource in a dedicated change what concerns pylintrc configuration  and enforce it in tox configuration by the way

https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/126240

IMO this new configuration file should better be stated explicitly ( with --rcfile option) both in the .gitlab-ci.yml  and tox.ini calls to pylint for 2 reasons
- to be sure it is taken into account correctly
  the home directory may vary depending the context and pylint takes by default $HOME/.pylintrc
- to avoid the parameters here are unadvertisely overriden by an option specifed manually in the pylint command call
Done

these options override most of the settings in the .pylintrc added by this change...
what can lead to manipulation errors
please remove them by rebasing on top of https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/126240
I don't know this distrib - sorry.

Fully misleading.

I clearly prefer the tox approach (interop', maintenance, security, etc.) and I don't see why GitlabCI would force this particular design ("one image per linter"). Removing tox would be more precise as title.
